a: An AI agent is a system built around a language model that can take actions to reach a goal, not just chat. It can decide what to do, use tools, and keep going step by step until the task is done.
fs: An agent is an LLM placed in a loop with tools and a goal, so it can act, not just answer.
fj: Thinking any chatbot is an agent. A plain LLM only replies with text; an agent actually does things.

q: What is a tool in an AI agent?
a: A tool is an outside ability the agent can use, like searching the web, running code, or calling an API. The model can't do these on its own, so we give it tools and let it choose when to use them.
fs: A tool is a named function with a description the model picks and fills in arguments for.
fj: Assuming the model runs the tool itself. The model only requests the call; your code actually runs it and returns the result.

a: The agent loop is the repeating cycle an agent runs: think about the goal, take an action, then look at the result. It repeats this think-act-observe cycle until the task is finished.
fs: The loop is Thought to Action to Observation, repeated until the goal is met (the ReAct pattern).
fj: Expecting one model call to finish everything. Agents solve hard tasks across many small looped steps.

q: What is memory in an AI agent?
a: Memory is how an agent holds on to information, like earlier steps in the task or facts it learned. Short-term memory is the current conversation; long-term memory stores things to recall later.
fs: Memory feeds past steps and saved facts back into the prompt so the agent stays consistent across turns.
fj: Believing the model remembers on its own. It forgets between calls unless you pass the history back in.

q: What is planning in an AI agent?
a: Planning is when the agent breaks a big goal into smaller steps before or while acting. This helps it stay organized and tackle complex tasks instead of guessing one move at a time.
fs: Planning turns a vague goal into an ordered list of steps the agent can execute and check off.
fj: Skipping planning and acting impulsively. Without a plan, agents wander, repeat work, or lose the goal.
